news_summary: Scientists have achieved a major breakthrough by miniaturizing a powerful laser onto a chip after two decades, while a remarkable 40-million-year-old ant fossil was discovered in Goethe's collection.
news_summary_fr: Les scientifiques ont réalisé une avancée majeure en miniaturisant un laser puissant sur une puce après deux décennies de travaux, tandis qu'un remarquable fossile de fourmi vieux de 40 millions d'années a été découvert dans la collection de Goethe.
news_summary_es: Los científicos han logrado un gran avance al miniaturizar un potente láser en un chip tras dos décadas de trabajo, mientras que en la colección de Goethe se ha descubierto un extraordinario fósil de hormiga de 40 millones de años de antigüedad.

articles.title: After 20 years, scientists finally shrink a powerful laser onto a chip
articles.summary: Researchers at EPFL have developed a chip-scale ultrafast laser that performs on par with traditional tabletop femtosecond lasers. The innovation could make advanced laser technologies far smaller, cheaper, and more accessible for applications ranging from medical diagnostics to atomic clocks.
articles.content:
For decades, ultrafast lasers have been among the most powerful tools in modern optics. Their pulses last just a few hundred femtoseconds, or quadrillionths of a second, enabling technologies ranging from precision manufacturing and eye surgery to optical frequency combs, the Nobel Prize-winning innovation that powers the world's most accurate optical atomic clocks.

Despite their importance, these lasers have largely remained large, costly systems that occupy entire optical tables.

Now, researchers led by Professor Tobias J. Kippenberg at EPFL have achieved a breakthrough that could dramatically shrink the technology. Writing in Nature, the team reports the first integrated ultrafast laser capable of matching the performance of traditional tabletop femtosecond lasers. The device delivers pulse energies of 1.05 nanojoules and pulse durations as short as 147 femtoseconds, all from a photonic chip.

Bringing Ultrafast Lasers to Photonic Chips

Photonic chips manipulate light using microscopic structures called waveguides that are etched into a wafer. In much the same way electronic chips direct electrical signals, photonic chips guide and process light.

These chips are already widely used in telecommunications and have helped miniaturize many optical technologies that previously required much larger equipment.

"For more than twenty years, a high-pulse-energy femtosecond laser on chip was widely regarded as a holy grail of integrated photonics," says Kippenberg. "Our result shows that it is not only possible, but that it can be achieved with a surprisingly elegant architecture that the integrated-photonics community had overlooked."

An Overlooked Laser Design Delivers

To accomplish the feat, the researchers adopted a laser architecture known as the Mamyshev oscillator, a design that had received relatively little attention in integrated photonics.

The system places a nonlinear waveguide between two optical filters, each of which transmits a different portion of the light spectrum. As an intense laser pulse travels through the waveguide, it expands into a broader range of colors. Part of that broadened pulse can then pass through both filters and continue circulating within the laser cavity.

Weaker light behaves differently. Because it does not broaden enough, it is blocked by the filters and removed from the cycle.

"This design is especially attractive because it does not require any component that is difficult to make on this erbium-doped silicon nitride chip," explains Zheru Qiu, a co-leading author of the paper.

According to Qiu, the design offers another major advantage. Photonic chips confine light to extremely small waveguides, causing light to interact strongly with itself. In many laser architectures, these nonlinear effects can destabilize the laser pulses. The Mamyshev oscillator, however, is far less susceptible to these problems, making it particularly well suited for integrated photonic devices.

Tiny Device, Big Potential

The laser cavity measures 42 centimeters in length, yet it can be folded onto a chip occupying roughly the area of a match head. This makes it dramatically smaller than conventional fiber-based ultrafast lasers.

Because photonic chips can be manufactured at wafer scale using methods similar to those employed for computer chips, more than 1,000 laser cavities could potentially be produced simultaneously. That manufacturing advantage could significantly reduce the cost of ultrafast lasers while expanding their availability for sensing, spectroscopy, and precision measurement applications.

"With kilowatt-level peak powers, the chip can drive demanding applications that have long depended on large, expensive laboratory lasers," says Qiu.

The researchers believe the technology could eventually lead to portable and affordable devices for detecting environmental pollutants, identifying hidden defects in materials, and performing medical diagnostics. It could also help pave the way for compact optical atomic clocks that may play important roles in future communications and navigation systems.

The work involved researchers from the EPFL Institute of Electrical and Microengineering and Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf (HZDR).
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough where researchers have miniaturized powerful ultrafast lasers onto photonic chips, making advanced laser technology more accessible, affordable, and portable. This innovation has broad implications across multiple fields including medical diagnostics, environmental sensing, precision manufacturing, and atomic clocks, representing a substantial positive real-world impact with detailed context and significance.
articles.personality_title: Scientists shrink powerful ultrafast laser onto a tiny chip
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Ultrafast lasers produce extremely short bursts of light that last only a few hundred femtoseconds (a quadrillionth of a second). These lasers are important tools used in eye surgery, precision manufacturing, and the world’s most accurate atomic clocks. Until now, these lasers have been large, expensive machines that fill entire tables.

**What happened** – Researchers at EPFL, led by Professor Tobias J. Kippenberg, created the first ultrafast laser on a photonic chip that performs just as well as the large traditional lasers. They used a special design called the Mamyshev oscillator, which lets the laser pulses stay powerful and stable on a tiny chip about the size of a match head. Their device can produce pulses as short as 147 femtoseconds.

**Impact** – This breakthrough means ultrafast lasers can become much smaller, cheaper, and easier to make. Because photonic chips are made like computer chips, thousands of these lasers could be produced at once. This could make advanced laser technology widely available for uses like medical tests, detecting pollution, checking materials for defects, and building compact atomic clocks.

**What's next step** – The researchers plan to explore how to use these chip-based lasers in real-world applications. This includes making portable devices for doctors, environmental monitors, and improving communication and navigation systems with tiny atomic clocks.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have successfully miniaturized powerful ultrafast lasers onto small chips, opening the door to affordable, portable laser technologies for many important uses.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques miniaturisent un laser ultrarapide puissant sur une puce
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les lasers ultrarapides produisent des éclairs de lumière extrêmement courts, durant seulement quelques centaines de femtosecondes (un quadrillionième de seconde). Ces lasers sont des outils importants utilisés en chirurgie oculaire, fabrication de précision et horloges atomiques très précises. Jusqu’à présent, ces lasers étaient de grandes machines coûteuses occupant toute une table.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’EPFL, dirigés par le professeur Tobias J. Kippenberg, ont créé le premier laser ultrarapide sur une puce photonique qui fonctionne aussi bien que les lasers traditionnels volumineux. Ils ont utilisé un design spécial appelé oscillateur Mamyshev, qui permet aux impulsions laser de rester puissantes et stables sur une petite puce de la taille d’une tête d’allumette. Leur appareil produit des impulsions aussi courtes que 147 femtosecondes.

**Impact** – Cette avancée signifie que les lasers ultrarapides peuvent devenir beaucoup plus petits, moins chers et plus faciles à fabriquer. Comme les puces photoniques se fabriquent comme des puces électroniques, des milliers de ces lasers pourraient être produits en même temps. Cela pourrait rendre la technologie laser avancée largement accessible pour des usages comme les tests médicaux, la détection de pollution, le contrôle des matériaux et la fabrication d’horloges atomiques compactes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient d’explorer l’utilisation de ces lasers sur puce dans des applications réelles. Cela inclut la création d’appareils portables pour les médecins, des moniteurs environnementaux et l’amélioration des systèmes de communication et de navigation avec de petites horloges atomiques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont réussi à miniaturiser des lasers ultrarapides puissants sur de petites puces, ouvrant la voie à des technologies laser abordables et portables pour de nombreux usages importants.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos miniaturizan un potente láser ultrarrápido en un chip pequeño
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los láseres ultrarrápidos producen destellos de luz extremadamente cortos que duran solo unos cientos de femtosegundos (una milbillonésima de segundo). Estos láseres son herramientas importantes usadas en cirugía ocular, fabricación de precisión y los relojes atómicos más precisos del mundo. Hasta ahora, estos láseres eran máquinas grandes y costosas que ocupaban mesas completas.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de EPFL, liderados por el profesor Tobias J. Kippenberg, crearon el primer láser ultrarrápido en un chip fotónico que funciona igual de bien que los láseres tradicionales grandes. Usaron un diseño especial llamado oscilador Mamyshev, que permite que los pulsos láser se mantengan potentes y estables en un chip pequeño del tamaño de la cabeza de una cerilla. Su dispositivo puede producir pulsos tan cortos como 147 femtosegundos.

**Impacto** – Este avance significa que los láseres ultrarrápidos pueden volverse mucho más pequeños, baratos y fáciles de fabricar. Como los chips fotónicos se fabrican como los chips electrónicos, se podrían producir miles de estos láseres al mismo tiempo. Esto podría hacer que la tecnología láser avanzada esté disponible para usos como pruebas médicas, detección de contaminación, revisión de materiales y construcción de relojes atómicos compactos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean explorar cómo usar estos láseres en chip en aplicaciones reales. Esto incluye hacer dispositivos portátiles para médicos, monitores ambientales y mejorar sistemas de comunicación y navegación con relojes atómicos pequeños.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos han miniaturizado con éxito potentes láseres ultrarrápidos en pequeños chips, abriendo la puerta a tecnologías láser asequibles y portátiles para muchos usos importantes.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a delicate photonic chip shaped like a glowing match head, intricately etched with tiny waveguides that channel vibrant, multicolored light pulses expanding and weaving between two translucent spectral filters, symbolizing the breakthrough miniaturization of ultrafast lasers onto a chip.

articles.title: Goethe never knew this 40-million-year-old ant was hidden in his collection
articles.summary: Scientists examining amber from Goethe’s personal collection discovered three hidden fossil insects, including an extinct ant preserved in extraordinary detail. Advanced 3D imaging allowed researchers to see not only the ant’s outer features but also structures inside its body. The findings offer new clues about the species’ biology and suggest it likely built large nests in trees.
articles.content:
Scientists have uncovered hidden fossil insects inside pieces of amber that once belonged to the famed German writer Johann Wolfgang von Goethe. Using advanced imaging technology, researchers were able to reveal creatures that had remained trapped inside the fossilized tree resin for tens of millions of years.

Goethe's amber collection, now housed at the Goethe National Museum and managed by the Klassik Stiftung Weimar, contains 40 pieces of Baltic amber. Two of those specimens turned out to contain fossilized animals that were nearly impossible to see with the naked eye because the amber pieces were never polished.

To investigate further, researchers from the University of Jena turned to modern scanning techniques. At the German Electron Synchrotron (DESY) in Hamburg, they used synchrotron micro computed tomography to create detailed three dimensional images of the fossils. The scans revealed three insects: a fungus gnat, a black fly, and an ancient ant.

Ancient Ant Reveals New Details

Among the discoveries, the ant attracted the most attention.

"The ant belongs to the extinct species †Ctenobethylus goepperti (Mayr, 1868), which is very common in amber," explains Bernhard Bock from the Phyletisches Museum of the University of Jena. "Thanks to its excellent preservation and the extensive investigations, however, we were able to describe it in greater detail than ever before and gain new information about the species and its relationships."

Because the specimen is so exceptionally preserved, scientists were able to examine features that had never been documented in such detail. The scans revealed fine body hairs on the worker ant and even allowed researchers to visualize internal skeletal structures within the head and thorax. These observations provide valuable new information about the anatomy and evolution of the species.

The research team also produced a complete digital reconstruction of the fossil.

"We have fully processed the specimen and, based on the newly acquired information, created a 3D reconstruction that is available online," says Daniel Tröger from the University of Jena. "This model helps colleagues worldwide to identify and compare further fossils of this species."

Comparisons with the modern ant genus Liometopum, which is found today in North America and warmer parts of Europe, offer clues about how the extinct species may have lived. Researchers believe the ancient ants likely built large nests in trees, which may help explain why they are so commonly preserved in amber.

Goethe's Connection to Amber

Although Goethe owned the amber specimens, he showed relatively little interest in amber itself beyond its optical properties. He even ground lenses from fossilized resin to study color effects as part of his work on color theory.

By Goethe's time, scientists had already begun studying amber and the fossils preserved inside it. Early scientific publications on the subject were available in his personal library. However, the broader scientific importance of these fossils had not yet become clear, and the discoveries being made today were far beyond what researchers of that era could have imagined.

"Goethe is regarded as the founder of morphology and would likely have been delighted to see how we were able to gain valuable insights in this field using entirely new methods," says Bernhard Bock. "At the same time, the results demonstrate the value of such historical collections. It is truly fascinating that an object originating from his hand and his era, when this science was just beginning, can still enrich us so much today."

The findings highlight how museum collections assembled centuries ago can continue to yield important scientific discoveries. Thanks to modern imaging techniques, objects that once seemed ordinary can still reveal hidden stories from Earth's distant past.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant scientific discovery enabled by advanced 3D imaging technology, revealing new anatomical details of a 40-million-year-old ant fossil. This breakthrough enhances understanding of extinct species and demonstrates the value of historical collections combined with modern technology, benefiting the scientific community and public knowledge.
articles.personality_title: Scientists uncover hidden 40-million-year-old ant in Goethe’s amber collection
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, a famous German writer, once collected pieces of amber, fossilized tree resin. His collection, now in a museum, includes 40 pieces of Baltic amber that had remained mostly unexplored for hidden fossils.

**What happened** – Scientists used advanced 3D scanning technology called synchrotron micro computed tomography at a research center in Germany to look inside these amber pieces. They discovered three tiny fossil insects, including an extinct ant species that was preserved in amazing detail. This technology allowed them to see not only the outside of the ant but also its internal body structures.

**Impact** – The ant, named †Ctenobethylus goepperti, was studied more closely than ever before. Scientists found tiny hairs on its body and even parts of its internal skeleton. These details give new information about how the ant lived and evolved. The researchers also created a full 3D digital model of the ant that anyone can access online. This helps other scientists compare fossils and learn more about ancient ants. They also think these ants built large nests in trees, which might explain why they are often found in amber.

**What’s next step** – The digital model will be shared with scientists worldwide to support further studies of ancient insects. Researchers plan to use these new insights to better understand how extinct ants lived and how they relate to ants alive today.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using modern 3D technology, scientists revealed a well-preserved 40-million-year-old ant inside Goethe’s amber, offering new knowledge about ancient insect life and the value of old collections.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent une fourmi vieille de 40 millions d’années dans l’ambre de Goethe
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, célèbre écrivain allemand, possédait une collection d’ambre, une résine d’arbre fossilisée. Cette collection, aujourd’hui au musée, contient 40 pièces d’ambre de la mer Baltique, peu explorées jusqu’à présent pour des fossiles cachés.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont utilisé une technologie avancée d’imagerie 3D appelée tomographie micro-ordinateur synchrotron dans un centre de recherche en Allemagne pour examiner l’intérieur de ces morceaux d’ambre. Ils ont découvert trois petits insectes fossiles, dont une fourmi éteinte conservée dans un détail exceptionnel. Cette technologie leur a permis de voir non seulement l’extérieur de la fourmi mais aussi ses structures internes.

**Impact** – La fourmi, nommée †Ctenobethylus goepperti, a été étudiée plus en détail que jamais. Les chercheurs ont trouvé de minuscules poils sur son corps ainsi que des parties de son squelette interne. Ces détails apportent de nouvelles informations sur la vie et l’évolution de cette espèce. Ils ont aussi créé un modèle numérique 3D complet de la fourmi accessible en ligne, aidant d’autres scientifiques à comparer des fossiles et mieux comprendre les fourmis anciennes. Ils pensent aussi que ces fourmis construisaient de grands nids dans les arbres, ce qui expliquerait leur présence fréquente dans l’ambre.

**Prochaine étape** – Ce modèle numérique sera partagé avec des scientifiques du monde entier pour soutenir de futures recherches sur les insectes anciens. Les chercheurs souhaitent utiliser ces nouvelles données pour mieux comprendre la vie des fourmis disparues et leur lien avec les fourmis actuelles.

**En une phrase** – Grâce à la technologie 3D moderne, des scientifiques ont révélé une fourmi vieille de 40 millions d’années dans l’ambre de Goethe, offrant de nouvelles connaissances sur la vie des insectes anciens et la valeur des anciennes collections.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren una hormiga de 40 millones de años oculta en el ámbar de Goethe
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, un famoso escritor alemán, tenía una colección de ámbar, resina de árbol fosilizada. Su colección, que ahora está en un museo, incluye 40 piezas de ámbar báltico que apenas se habían explorado en busca de fósiles escondidos.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos usaron una tecnología avanzada de imágenes 3D llamada tomografía micro computarizada con sincrotrón en un centro de investigación de Alemania para examinar el interior de estos ámbares. Descubrieron tres pequeños insectos fósiles, incluyendo una especie de hormiga extinta conservada con gran detalle. Esta tecnología les permitió ver no solo el exterior de la hormiga, sino también sus estructuras internas.

**Impacto** – La hormiga, llamada †Ctenobethylus goepperti, fue estudiada con más detalle que nunca. Los científicos encontraron pequeños pelos en su cuerpo y partes de su esqueleto interno. Estos detalles brindan nueva información sobre cómo vivía y evolucionaba esta especie. También crearon un modelo digital 3D completo de la hormiga que está disponible en línea, ayudando a otros científicos a comparar fósiles y aprender más sobre hormigas antiguas. Además, creen que estas hormigas construían grandes nidos en árboles, lo que podría explicar por qué se encuentran frecuentemente en ámbar.

**Próximo paso** – El modelo digital será compartido con científicos de todo el mundo para apoyar futuros estudios de insectos antiguos. Los investigadores planean usar esta nueva información para entender mejor cómo vivían las hormigas extintas y su relación con las hormigas actuales.

**Frase principal** – Gracias a la tecnología 3D moderna, científicos revelaron una hormiga bien conservada de 40 millones de años en el ámbar de Goethe, aportando nuevo conocimiento sobre la vida de insectos antiguos y el valor de colecciones históricas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a translucent piece of Baltic amber glowing softly, revealing a finely detailed ancient ant with delicate body hairs and internal structures visible inside, surrounded by subtle representations of a vintage library shelf and old scientific instruments in soft natural tones, symbolizing the fusion of historic discovery and modern technology.
